expanded_description: Quantitative evidence on the SAT appears in the form of graphs, tables, charts, and data-based passages that require students to extract, interpret, and apply numerical information. Unlike textual evidence, which is evaluated through language and logic, quantitative evidence demands visual and numerical literacy: the ability to read axes and labels, identify trends and patterns, compute simple relationships, and connect data points to claims. The SAT tests these skills both in the Reading section, where students must interpret data presented alongside informational passages, and in the Writing and Language section, where students must evaluate whether claims are supported by the accompanying data graphics. Mastering quantitative evidence requires students to approach data methodically, translate visual information into verbal statements, and apply the same rigorous standards of relevance and support that govern textual evidence.

core_concept.principle:
Quantitative evidence is data presented in structured visual or numerical formats—graphs, tables, charts, and infographics—that is offered to support or illustrate a claim. Interpreting quantitative evidence requires a systematic approach: first, understand what is being measured and how it is being displayed; second, extract the specific data points or trends relevant to the claim; third, translate the numerical or visual information into a precise verbal statement that can be tested against the claim for logical support.

The systematic interpretation of quantitative evidence involves four essential steps:

(1) Orient to the display by reading the title, axis labels, units of measurement, and any footnotes or source information. Without understanding what the numbers represent and the scale on which they are measured, no interpretation is possible. Every element of a graph or table conveys information that is necessary for accurate reading.

(2) Identify the overall pattern by noting the general trend, distribution, or relationship the data shows. Is there an increase, a decrease, a correlation, a clustering, an outlier? What is the shape of the data before zooming into specific values?

(3) Locate the specific data points or comparisons relevant to the claim being evaluated. A claim about 'the year with the highest value' requires finding the maximum; a claim about 'the difference between two groups' requires extracting both values and computing the difference.

(4) Translate the visual or numerical finding into a precise verbal statement. 'The bar for 2020 is twice as high as the bar for 2010' becomes 'The value in 2020 was approximately double the value in 2010.' This verbal statement can then be tested against the claim for directness, completeness, and accuracy of support.
core_concept.explanation:
Quantitative evidence is a foreign language to many students who are comfortable with words but uneasy with numbers. The SAT exploits this discomfort by presenting data graphics that, upon calm and methodical inspection, are straightforward, but under time pressure can appear intimidating. The key insight is that quantitative evidence on the SAT Reading section does not require advanced mathematical computation. The math involved is almost always limited to reading values, comparing magnitudes, and recognizing basic trends. The real challenge is attentional and interpretive: the student must slow down, read the display carefully, and avoid the snap judgments that lead to misreading axes or confusing similar-looking data series.

Consider a common error: a student glances at a line graph showing two lines—one rising steeply, one rising gently—and concludes that 'the top line is much larger than the bottom line.' But the axis reveals that the top line represents values in the range of 10-20 while the bottom line represents values in the range of 100-200. The 'top' line is visually higher because of how the graph is scaled, but in absolute terms, the 'bottom' line represents values roughly ten times larger. The student has confused visual position with absolute magnitude, a mistake that would not occur if the student had methodically read the axis labels before interpreting the data. This is the quantitative equivalent of the 'topically adjacent but logically irrelevant' trap: the visual impression is cognitively salient but factually misleading.

Another critical skill is recognizing when data does not support a claim, even if the claim and the data share the same topic. A claim that 'traffic accidents have increased dramatically' paired with a table showing that 'traffic accidents rose from 1,000 to 1,050 over five years' is not well-supported—the data shows a modest increase, not a dramatic one. The evidence does not match the claim's characterization. Quantitative evidence evaluation thus requires not only reading the numbers correctly but also assessing whether the magnitude, direction, and nature of the data actually correspond to the claim's description of reality.

image.alt: An annotated sample graph showing a title, x-axis with units, y-axis with units, two data series with a legend, and callouts pointing to each element with instructions: 'Read title first,' 'Check axis scales,' 'Note units,' 'Compare series carefully'
image.caption: Systematic interpretation of quantitative evidence begins with orienting to the display: title, axes, units, and legend must all be read before drawing conclusions

sections.section_title: Foundation: Types of Quantitative Displays and How to Read Them
sections.introduction: Before students can use quantitative evidence to support or evaluate claims, they must become fluent in reading the types of data displays that appear on the SAT. This section introduces the most common formats—bar graphs, line graphs, pie charts, and tables—and provides a systematic reading protocol for each. Students will learn to identify what each display type is best suited to communicate and what common misreadings to avoid.

key_concept.definition:
Each type of quantitative display has a specific communicative purpose and a specific set of conventions for reading it correctly. The skilled reader recognizes the display type immediately and applies the appropriate reading protocol.

Bar graphs compare values across discrete categories. The x-axis shows categories (e.g., years, countries, treatment groups); the y-axis shows a numerical value. The height of each bar represents the value for that category. Bar graphs are designed to facilitate direct comparison between categories.

Line graphs show change over time or across a continuous variable. The x-axis shows the continuous variable (almost always time on the SAT); the y-axis shows the measured value. The slope of the line indicates the rate of change; the overall shape indicates the trend pattern.

Pie charts show proportions of a whole. Each slice represents a category's share of the total, expressed as a percentage. The entire circle always represents 100%. Pie charts are designed to communicate relative size within a fixed total.

Tables display numerical data organized in rows and columns. They allow for precise value lookup and comparison across multiple variables simultaneously. Unlike graphs, tables do not provide a visual impression of magnitude; the reader must extract and compare the numbers directly.
key_concept.context: On the SAT, quantitative displays are always accompanied by a prose passage that makes claims based on the data. The student's task is to verify whether the data shown actually supports the claims made. This requires precise reading of both the display and the claim, and a careful comparison between them.

categories.description: How to read bar graphs accurately and avoid common interpretation errors.

examples.explanation:
(1) Read the title to understand the overall subject of the data.

(2) Read the x-axis label to identify what the bars represent (categories, groups, time periods).

(3) Read the y-axis label to identify what is being measured and in what units. Pay careful attention to the scale—does the y-axis start at zero, or is it truncated? A truncated y-axis can visually exaggerate differences. Always check the numerical values, not just the visual heights.

(4) If there are multiple bar sets (grouped or stacked), read the legend to understand what each color or pattern represents.

(5) When comparing values, read the exact number from the top of the bar or from the y-axis gridlines. Estimate if the bar falls between gridlines.

Example: A bar graph titled 'Average Daily Commute Time by City (2023)' with the x-axis showing city names (Tokyo, London, New York, Mumbai) and the y-axis showing 'Minutes (0-80).' The Tokyo bar reaches 60, London reaches 45, New York reaches 52, and Mumbai reaches 75. A claim that 'Mumbai had the longest average commute among the four cities in 2023' is supported by the graph—the Mumbai bar is clearly the tallest, at 75 minutes. A claim that 'New York's commute time was more than twice London's' is not supported—52 is not more than twice 45 (which would be 90).

Error 1: Confusing the categories on the x-axis. A student might compare 'London' and 'New York' and accidentally read the wrong bars. Always trace from the category label straight up to the top of the corresponding bar.

Error 2: Misreading the y-axis scale. If the y-axis increments by 20 (0, 20, 40, 60, 80), a bar reaching halfway between 40 and 60 represents 50, not 100. Always check the interval between tick marks.

Error 3: Ignoring a truncated y-axis. If the y-axis starts at 50 instead of 0, a bar that appears twice as tall as another may represent a much smaller proportional difference. Always check the numerical starting point of the axis.

categories.description: How to read line graphs accurately and interpret slopes, inflections, and intersections.

examples.explanation:
(1) Read the title to understand the overall subject.

(2) Read the x-axis to identify the continuous variable, which is almost always time on the SAT (years, months, days). Note the interval and the total span.

(3) Read the y-axis to identify what is being measured and in what units. As with bar graphs, check the scale and starting point carefully.

(4) If there are multiple lines, read the legend to identify each one. Trace each line with your finger or your eyes to connect the legend label to the line on the graph.

(5) When interpreting trends, note the direction (increasing, decreasing, stable), the steepness (steep slope = rapid change; shallow slope = slow change), and any inflection points where the direction or steepness changes significantly.

(6) When two lines are shown, note their relationship: Do they move together (correlated)? Does one consistently exceed the other? Do they cross at any point, and if so, when?

Example: A line graph titled 'Monthly Sales Revenue, 2023' with the x-axis showing months (Jan-Dec) and the y-axis showing 'Thousands of Dollars (0-100).' One line labeled 'Online Sales' rises steadily from 20 in January to 60 in December. A second line labeled 'In-Store Sales' oscillates between 40 and 50 all year. A claim that 'Online sales grew steadily throughout the year while in-store sales remained relatively flat' is supported. A claim that 'Online sales surpassed in-store sales in June' requires checking the intersection: if the online line crosses above the in-store line between May and June, the claim is supported. If the lines cross in August, the claim about June is not supported.

Error 1: Confusing which line is which. The legend may use similar colors or patterns. Methodically trace from the legend label to the specific line on the graph before making any interpretations.

Error 2: Mistaking a steep visual slope for a large absolute change. A steep slope on a graph with a y-axis scaled from 0 to 1000 may represent a small proportional change. A shallow slope on a graph with a y-axis scaled from 0 to 10 may represent a dramatic change. Always read the actual values at the start and end points.

Error 3: Extrapolating beyond the data. If the x-axis ends at December 2023, the graph provides no information about January 2024. Claims about future trends are not supported by the displayed data alone.

categories.description: How to navigate tables efficiently and extract the specific values needed for comparison.

examples.explanation:
(1) Read the title to understand what data the table contains.

(2) Identify the row and column headers. What does each row represent? What does each column represent? What are the units for each column?

(3) When a question asks about a specific value, locate the correct intersection of row and column. Use your finger or a mental pointer to trace across and down to the exact cell.

(4) When a question asks about a comparison, extract all relevant values before making the comparison. Do not try to hold numbers in your head—write them down or mark them clearly.

(5) Check for footnotes that may qualify the data (e.g., 'Values adjusted for inflation,' 'Sample size = 500'). Footnotes provide essential context for accurate interpretation.

Example: A table titled 'Unemployment Rate by Education Level, 2020-2023' with rows for 'Less than High School,' 'High School Graduate,' 'Some College,' 'Bachelor's Degree,' and 'Graduate Degree,' and columns for each year 2020 through 2023. A claim that 'Workers with a bachelor's degree had lower unemployment rates than those with only a high school diploma in every year shown' requires checking all four pairs of values. If the bachelor's row shows 4.2, 3.8, 3.1, 2.9 and the high school row shows 7.1, 6.5, 5.8, 5.2, the claim is supported—bachelor's is lower in all four years. A claim that 'The unemployment rate for high school graduates fell by more than two percentage points between 2020 and 2023' requires computing 7.1 minus 5.2, which equals 1.9 percentage points. The claim says 'more than two,' but the actual decline is 1.9. The claim is not supported.

Error 1: Reading the wrong row or column. A student might compare the 'Some College' row to the 'High School' row when the claim is about 'Bachelor's Degree.' Always double-check that you are reading the correct category.

Error 2: Confusing percentage points with percent change. A decline from 7.1% to 5.2% is a decline of 1.9 percentage points. As a percent change, it is approximately a 27% decline. The SAT may test this distinction. Claims about 'percentage points' require subtraction; claims about 'percent change' require division.

Error 3: Overlooking table footnotes. An asterisk or superscript number may indicate that certain values are estimated, provisional, or adjusted. Claims that do not account for these qualifications may be unsupported or only partially supported.

worked_examples.title: Interpreting a Compound Data Display
worked_examples.problem:
Analyze the following data display and evaluate which of the claims are supported by the data.

Table: 'Global Renewable Energy Consumption by Source, 2015 vs. 2022 (in Terawatt-Hours)'
| Source | 2015 | 2022 | Percent Change |
|--------|------|------|----------------|
| Solar | 250 | 1,300 | +420% |
| Wind | 830 | 2,100 | +153% |
| Hydro | 3,900 | 4,400 | +13% |
| Other | 420 | 600 | +43% |
| Total | 5,400 | 8,400 | +56% |

Claims to Evaluate:
A) 'Solar energy consumption more than quintupled between 2015 and 2022.'
B) 'Wind energy remained the second-largest renewable source throughout the period.'
C) 'Hydroelectric power accounted for the largest absolute increase in consumption of any single source.'
D) 'The share of total renewable energy coming from solar power approximately tripled.'

step_by_step.explanation: The table shows renewable energy consumption by source in terawatt-hours for two years, 2015 and 2022, with a calculated percent change column. The 'Total' row at the bottom provides the sum across all sources. Units are terawatt-hours (TWh), a standard energy measurement.

Claim A: 'Solar energy consumption more than quintupled between 2015 and 2022.' Quintupled means multiplied by five. Solar in 2015 was 250 TWh. Five times 250 is 1,250 TWh. Actual 2022 solar is 1,300 TWh. 1,300 is greater than 1,250. The claim is supported—solar more than quintupled. The percent change column confirms this: +420% means the value in 2022 is the 2015 value plus 420% of the 2015 value, which equals 520% of the 2015 value, or 5.2 times the original. 5.2 is greater than 5, so 'more than quintupled' is accurate.

Claim B: 'Wind energy remained the second-largest renewable source throughout the period.' In 2015, the ranking by consumption was: Hydro (3,900 TWh), Wind (830 TWh), Other (420 TWh), Solar (250 TWh). Wind was second. In 2022, the ranking was: Hydro (4,400 TWh), Wind (2,100 TWh), Solar (1,300 TWh), Other (600 TWh). Wind was still second. The claim is supported. Wind remained in second place in both years. Note that the order of Other and Solar switched, but Wind's position did not change.

Claim C: 'Hydroelectric power accounted for the largest absolute increase in consumption of any single source.' Absolute increase means the raw numerical difference between 2022 and 2015 values. Compute each: Hydro: 4,400 - 3,900 = 500 TWh increase. Wind: 2,100 - 830 = 1,270 TWh increase. Solar: 1,300 - 250 = 1,050 TWh increase. Other: 600 - 420 = 180 TWh increase. Wind had the largest absolute increase (1,270 TWh), not hydro (500 TWh). The claim is not supported. Hydro had the largest total consumption in both years but not the largest increase. This illustrates the important distinction between absolute value and rate of change—a common SAT trap.

Claim D: 'The share of total renewable energy coming from solar power approximately tripled.' Share refers to solar's proportion of the total. In 2015, solar share was 250 / 5,400 = approximately 0.0463, or about 4.6%. In 2022, solar share was 1,300 / 8,400 = approximately 0.1548, or about 15.5%. The share in 2022 divided by the share in 2015 is 15.5% / 4.6% = approximately 3.37. The share roughly tripled (multiplied by about 3.4). The claim that it 'approximately tripled' is supported. This is different from Claim A, which was about the absolute amount (which quintupled). The share triples while the absolute amount quintuples because the total also grew significantly. This distinction between absolute change and share change is frequently tested.

worked_examples.final_answer: Claims A, B, and D are supported by the data. Claim C is not supported—wind, not hydro, had the largest absolute increase. This analysis demonstrates several critical quantitative reasoning skills: distinguishing absolute change from percent change (Claim C vs. Claim A), computing proportions and their change over time (Claim D), and verifying rankings across multiple time points (Claim B).
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example integrates multiple quantitative reasoning skills: reading values from a table, computing differences and proportions, distinguishing between absolute and relative measures, and verifying claims against precise numerical data. The most important discipline is to compute the relevant quantity before evaluating the claim, rather than relying on visual or intuitive impressions.

image.alt: Four panels showing the four display types—bar graph, line graph, pie chart, table—each with annotations indicating the key reading protocol steps for that type
image.caption: Each quantitative display type requires a specific reading protocol, but all share the same fundamental process: orient, identify patterns, locate specific values, and translate into precise verbal statements

sections.section_title: Application: Evaluating Claims Against Quantitative Evidence
sections.introduction: This section provides practice with the specific format of SAT questions that require students to evaluate whether a claim is supported by an accompanying quantitative display. Students will work through passages paired with data graphics and apply the systematic interpretation protocol to determine whether the data supports, undermines, or is irrelevant to each claim.

key_concept.definition:
On the SAT, quantitative evidence questions typically present a prose passage alongside a data graphic. The passage makes claims that the student must verify against the graphic. The correct answer to an evidence question will be the choice that accurately describes what the data shows and directly supports the claim in question. Incorrect choices often misrepresent the data—stating a value that does not appear, describing a trend that does not exist, or drawing a conclusion that exceeds what the data can support.

The evaluation process is: (1) Read the claim in the passage carefully. (2) Locate the relevant part of the data graphic. (3) Extract the precise data needed. (4) Compare the data to the claim. (5) Determine whether the data supports the claim, contradicts it, or is insufficient to evaluate it.
key_concept.context: These questions test the integration of textual and quantitative information—the ability to move fluidly between prose claims and numerical evidence. This is a real-world skill essential for academic research, professional analysis, and informed citizenship.

categories.description: A passage paired with a graph, followed by claims that must be checked against the data.

examples.explanation:
Passage Excerpt: 'The growth of remote work has reshaped American migration patterns since 2020. While early predictions suggested a mass exodus from expensive coastal cities to affordable inland areas, the reality has been more nuanced. According to the data, remote work adoption has been associated with movement out of very high-cost cities, but the primary beneficiaries have been medium-sized cities and suburban areas within the same region, rather than entirely different parts of the country. The data also reveals significant demographic variation: workers under 35 have been far more likely to relocate than older workers, and households with children have moved at nearly twice the rate of households without.'

Data Graphic Description: A bar graph titled 'Remote Worker Relocation Rate by Destination Type and Age Group, 2020-2023.' The x-axis has three categories: 'Moved Within Same Metro Area,' 'Moved to Different Metro Area, Same State,' and 'Moved to Different State.' Within each category, there are two bars side-by-side: one for 'Under 35' and one for '35 and Over.' The y-axis shows 'Percentage of Remote Workers Who Relocated (0-35%).' The values are approximately: Within Same Metro Area: Under 35 (22%), 35 and Over (12%); Different Metro, Same State: Under 35 (18%), 35 and Over (10%); Different State: Under 35 (8%), 35 and Over (5%).

Claim 1 (from the passage): 'Workers under 35 have been far more likely to relocate than older workers.' Evaluation: For each destination type, the under-35 bar is higher than the 35-and-over bar. Total relocation rates can be approximated by summing: Under 35 total is approximately 22 + 18 + 8 = 48%. Over 35 total is approximately 12 + 10 + 5 = 27%. The under-35 rate is roughly 1.8 times the over-35 rate. Is this 'far more likely'? The difference is substantial—a 21 percentage-point gap and a ratio of nearly 2 to 1. This is a reasonable characterization as 'far more likely.' The data supports the claim.

Claim 2 (from the passage): 'The primary beneficiaries have been medium-sized cities and suburban areas within the same region, rather than entirely different parts of the country.' Evaluation: The data shows that the two highest relocation categories across both age groups are 'Within Same Metro Area' (22% and 12%) and 'Different Metro, Same State' (18% and 10%). 'Different State' has the lowest rates (8% and 5%). This means that when remote workers relocate, they are far more likely to stay within their current state—either in the same metro area or in a different metro area within the same state—than to move to a different state. This supports the claim that the primary movement is within the same region rather than to entirely different parts of the country. The data supports the claim.

Claim 3: 'Households with children have moved at nearly twice the rate of households without.'

Evaluation: The data graphic shows breakdowns by age group (Under 35 vs. 35 and Over) but does not provide any data on household composition—whether households have children or not. The claim about children cannot be evaluated using this graphic. The evidence is not present. If this were an SAT question asking whether the graphic supports this claim, the answer would be that the graphic does not provide the needed data. This illustrates a critical skill: recognizing when data is insufficient to evaluate a claim, even when the data is topically related.

examples.process: Common Quantitative Trap: The Mismatched Variable
examples.explanation: Claim 3 represents one of the most common quantitative evidence traps on the SAT: the claim references a variable (household composition) that is not measured in the given data display (which measures age group). Students who are not reading the data graphic's labels carefully may assume that because the passage discusses households with children, the graphic must show that data. But the graphic only shows what its axes and legend indicate—nothing more. Verifying that the data graphic actually contains the variable referenced in the claim is the first and most fundamental step in quantitative evidence evaluation.

image.alt: A sample SAT page layout with a prose passage on the left and a data graphic on the right, with arrows connecting specific claims in the passage to specific features of the graphic for verification
image.caption: SAT quantitative evidence questions require fluid movement between prose claims and the specific data features that support or fail to support them

summary:
Quantitative evidence on the SAT requires students to interpret data presented in visual and numerical formats—graphs, tables, and charts—and to evaluate whether claims made in accompanying passages are supported by that data. This skill integrates visual literacy, numerical reasoning, and textual analysis into a single competency that is tested throughout the Reading section and the Writing and Language section.

The systematic interpretation of any quantitative display follows four steps:

(1) Orient to the display by reading the title, axis labels, units, legend, and any footnotes. Every element of the display conveys essential information that must be understood before interpretation begins.

(2) Identify the overall pattern—the general trend, distribution, or relationship the data shows. What is the shape and direction of the data before examining specific values?

(3) Locate the specific data points or comparisons relevant to the claim being evaluated. A claim about a maximum requires finding the highest value; a claim about a difference requires extracting both values and computing the difference.

(4) Translate the visual or numerical finding into a precise verbal statement that can be tested against the claim for accuracy and support.

Each type of quantitative display has a specific communicative purpose and a specific reading protocol. Bar graphs compare values across discrete categories; the reader must check the y-axis scale carefully, including whether it starts at zero, to avoid being misled by visual proportions. Line graphs show trends over time; the reader must attend to slope, inflection points, and intersections between multiple lines, while avoiding extrapolation beyond the displayed time period. Pie charts show proportions of a whole; the reader must verify that percentages sum correctly and that the whole is properly defined. Tables provide precise numerical values for multiple variables; the reader must locate the correct row-column intersection and check for footnotes that qualify the data.

The most common quantitative reasoning errors on the SAT include confusing absolute change with percent change (a decline of 2 percentage points is not the same as a 2% decline), mistaking visual position for absolute magnitude when axes are scaled differently, extrapolating trends beyond the displayed data, and—most critically—attempting to verify a claim using a data graphic that does not contain the relevant variable. This last error is the quantitative equivalent of the irrelevant evidence trap: the data graphic may be on the same general topic as the claim, but if it does not measure the specific variable the claim addresses, it cannot serve as evidence for or against that claim.

Mastery of quantitative evidence requires the same logical discipline that governs textual evidence evaluation: precise reading, careful comparison, and a refusal to be satisfied with vague or approximate matches between data and claim. The data says exactly what it says, within the limits of its measurement and display. The skilled reader respects those limits, extracts the data accurately, and insists that claims be held accountable to the numerical reality the data represents.
